explanations.64: The function adds a leading slash if the joined path doesn't already have one, ensuring consistent URL formatting.
explanations.78: This removes all trailing slashes from the site URL, then concatenates it with the full path to create a complete absolute URL.
explanations.87: We extract the file extension from the URL and convert it to lowercase so we can match it against known image formats.
explanations.94: AVIF images are treated as PNG MIME type here, which is a simplification—AVIF should ideally return 'image/avif' but some older systems may not recognize it.
explanations.117: This regex removes invisible control characters and special Unicode characters that could break XML parsing, using hexadecimal character codes.
explanations.127: This selector uses the custom :contains() pseudo-selector to find headings that have the exact text 'Related posts', which cheerio can handle.
explanations.128: The :has() pseudo-selector finds a div with class 'row' that contains a child div with classes 'col' and 'col--4'.
explanations.159: Since the HTML structure might vary, if we don't find an <article> tag, we try alternative selectors that Docusaurus commonly uses.
explanations.176: We're finding code snippet wrapper elements and replacing the wrapper with just the inner code block to clean up the RSS output.
explanations.195: We remove leading and trailing slashes from the slug attribute because URL paths shouldn't have those characters on the edges.
explanations.203: For files named 'index.md' inside a folder, we use the folder name as the slug instead of the word 'index' itself.
explanations.231: We dynamically import the 'feed' library here (instead of at the top) because it's only needed inside this function when the build actually runs.
explanations.258: We parse the YAML file into a JavaScript object so we can look up author data by their ID keys later.
explanations.275: If no explicit date is set in front matter, we fall back to using the file's creation date from the filesystem.
explanations.317: We sort posts in descending order so the newest articles appear first in the RSS feed.
explanations.322: We ensure maxItems is at least 1 and convert it to a number, in case someone passes an invalid value.
explanations.337: We check if authors is already an array; if not but it exists, we wrap it in an array so the code below always works with an array.
explanations.349: We build a custom XML string with CDATA sections manually because the 'feed' library doesn't natively support dc:creator with CDATA wrapping.
explanations.403: The spread operator here conditionally adds the 'enclosure' property only if all three conditions are true (image enabled, URL exists, and MIME type found).
explanations.429: We load the RSS XML into Cheerio in 'xmlMode' so it properly parses XML structure rather than treating it as HTML.
explanations.432: We add the Dublin Core namespace declaration to the root <rss> element so the XML knows what 'dc:' means.
explanations.436: We select all <item> elements from the RSS structure to match them with our source data array by index.
explanations.444: Since the 'feed' library generates XML items in the same order as our array, we iterate by index to match each source item with its generated XML element.
explanations.474: We replace the auto-generated XML declaration with a custom one that also includes a stylesheet reference so RSS readers can apply better formatting.
